title: Quiz: Containerization for Quantum Applications
description: Test your understanding of Docker and container concepts for quantum software deployment

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using multi-stage Docker builds for quantum applications?

options.text: They make the application run faster
They separate build tools from runtime, reducing image size and attack surface
They allow running multiple applications in one container
They automatically optimize quantum circuits

questions.explanation: Multi-stage builds separate the build environment (with compilers, dev tools) from the runtime environment. This results in smaller images that only contain what's needed to run the application, reducing both size and security vulnerabilities.

questions.question: How should IBM Quantum credentials be handled in Docker containers?

options.text: Hardcode them in the Dockerfile for convenience
Store them in a config file inside the image
Inject them at runtime via environment variables or mounted secrets
Include them in the requirements.txt file

questions.explanation: Credentials should never be baked into Docker images as they persist in image layers and can be extracted. Instead, inject them at runtime using environment variables, Docker secrets, or mounted credential files.

questions.question: Why is it recommended to create a non-root user in Docker containers for quantum applications?

options.text: To make quantum simulations run faster
To reduce the potential impact of container breakout vulnerabilities
Because Python only works with non-root users
To enable GPU access

questions.explanation: Running as a non-root user follows the principle of least privilege. If a container is compromised, the attacker has limited permissions, reducing the potential damage from container escape vulnerabilities.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the HEALTHCHECK instruction in a Dockerfile?

options.text: To install health monitoring software
To define how Docker tests if the container is working correctly
To check the health of quantum hardware
To monitor network connectivity

questions.explanation: HEALTHCHECK defines a command that Docker runs periodically to check if the container is functioning correctly. This enables orchestrators like Kubernetes to detect and replace unhealthy containers automatically.

questions.question: In a Docker Compose setup for quantum development, why would you mount the source directory as a volume?

options.text: To make the image smaller
To enable hot reload during development without rebuilding the image
To improve security
To enable GPU acceleration

questions.explanation: Mounting the source directory as a volume allows developers to see code changes immediately without rebuilding the Docker image. This significantly speeds up the development workflow.

questions.question: Which of the following are security best practices for quantum application containers? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Pin base image versions
Run as root for full access
Scan images for vulnerabilities
Never embed secrets in images

questions.explanation: Security best practices include pinning base image versions (reproducibility), scanning for vulnerabilities, and never embedding secrets. Running as root is NOT a best practice - always use non-root users.

questions.question: What is the benefit of using an entrypoint script in a quantum application container?

options.text: It makes the container start faster
It allows dynamic configuration setup (like credential files) before the main process starts
It's required for Python applications
It enables quantum hardware access

questions.explanation: An entrypoint script runs before the main command and can perform setup tasks like configuring credentials from environment variables, checking prerequisites, or adjusting configuration based on the runtime environment.

questions.question: When deploying quantum services to Kubernetes, what resource should be used to store IBM Quantum tokens?

options.text: ConfigMap
PersistentVolume
Secret
Deployment annotation

questions.explanation: Kubernetes Secrets are designed for storing sensitive information like tokens and passwords. They are base64-encoded and can be mounted as files or exposed as environment variables with proper access controls.
